metadata.summary: The design should speak the users' language, with words, phrases, and concepts familiar to the user, rather than internal jargon. Follow real-world conventions, making information appear in a natural and logical order.

content.title: Match Between System and Real World
content.summary: Users encounter unfamiliar jargon and technical terms. Icons and labels do not match what users expect. Information is organized in system-centric rather than user-centric ways. Concepts from the real world are misrepresented or ignored. Without matching the real world: user confusion, learning curve increases, errors from misunderstanding, reduced adoption.

why.p: Usability Heuristic #2: The design should speak the users' language, with words, phrases, and concepts familiar to the user, rather than internal jargon. Follow real-world conventions, making information appear in a natural and logical order.

list.item: Content audit: reviewing all UI labels, error messages, and instructions for technical language
Localization prep: ensuring terminology matches user vocabulary before translation
Error message redesign: replacing system-generated error codes with plain language
Onboarding copy review: new users encounter the most jargon because they lack domain context
B2B product launches into a new industry vertical: the same feature may need entirely different terminology for finance vs. healthcare vs. logistics users
Developer tools and CLIs intentionally targeted at technical users — technical language is correct for the audience
Internal admin panels where all users are trained staff familiar with system terminology
When users have explicitly indicated they prefer technical precision over simplicity (e.g., API documentation)

section.name: language-guidelines
section.p: Key principles for matching real-world language:

list.item: Use familiar language - Words users actually use
Follow conventions - Match real-world expectations
Logical information flow - Natural order for users
Meaningful metaphors - Connect digital to physical

rule.statement: Use user-friendly language in place of technical jargon: "Log out" instead of "Terminate session", "Search" instead of "Execute query", "Please enter a valid email" instead of "Invalid input", "Folder / Collection" instead of "Repository", "Sign in" instead of "Authenticate", "Update / Refresh" instead of "Synchronize".
rule.rationale: Users search and think in everyday language. Technical terminology creates a barrier to entry and forces users to learn internal system concepts before accomplishing their actual goal.

list.item: E-commerce: Shopping cart, checkout, wishlist
Documents: Files, folders, save, open
Email: Inbox, sent, draft, trash
Social: Friends, followers, like, share
Navigation: Home, back, menu, search

section.name: implementation-patterns
section.p: Practical patterns for implementing real-world language:

rule.statement: Error messages must explain what happened in plain language (no error codes or HTTP status), tell the user what to do next, and match the intended tone (friendly/professional/minimal).
rule.rationale: Error messages occur at moments of user frustration. A technical error code adds confusion; plain language + next steps restores user agency and reduces support load.

example.title: Natural Language in Error Messages
example.code: Bad: "Error 403: Authentication failed" Good: "Incorrect password. Please try again."

example.title: Natural Language in Instructions
example.code: Bad: "Input alphanumeric characters only" Good: "Use only letters and numbers"

rule.statement: Logical information order must follow how the user thinks, not how the system stores data. Address forms: Street → City → State → Zip → Country (user mental model), not Country → State → City → Street → Zip (database schema).
rule.rationale: Users have a cognitive map of how things are organized in the real world. Forcing them to learn the system's organization creates confusion and errors.

example.title: Logical Address Form Order
example.code: Bad: Country > State > City > Street > Zip Good: Street > City > State > Zip > Country (Matches how people think about addresses)

section.note: Date selection varies by locale: US = Month / Day / Year; Europe = Day / Month / Year. Match user's locale.

rule.statement: Icons must be paired with text labels (or tooltips if space-constrained). Icon-only controls force users to recall meaning; recognition is easier than recall.
rule.rationale: Even if a metaphor is outdated (e.g., floppy disk for save), users recognize it. But unfamiliar icons are pure memorization burden.

list.item: Save: Floppy disk (legacy) or cloud
Delete: Trash can
Settings: Gear
Search: Magnifying glass
Home: House
Close: X
File management: Desktop = workspace, Folders = organization, Trash = deletion (recoverable)
E-commerce: Cart = collecting items, Checkout = payment area, Wishlist = items for later

rule.statement: Plain-language alternatives must be sourced from support tickets and user interview transcripts, not from the team's internal vocabulary. Do not guess.
rule.rationale: Plausible-sounding terminology chosen by designers often differs from what users actually search for and use. Research is the only reliable input.

list.item: User interviews - Listen to how they describe tasks
Support tickets - What terms do users use?
Search logs - What do they search for?
Card sorting - How do they categorize?
Competitor analysis - Industry standard terms
A/B testing - Different label variations
Comprehension testing - Can users explain what it means?
Task completion - Does terminology help or hinder?

antipattern.reason: Users have no context for internal terminology; it creates a barrier to entry.

antipattern.description: Technical language: Developer/system terminology in user-facing copy
antipattern.reason: Not all users are technical; the feature must be usable by the broadest intended audience.
Abbreviations require recall; spelled-out terms are more discoverable in search and easier to understand.

antipattern.description: Inconsistent terms: Same thing called different names
antipattern.reason: Inconsistency forces users to recall multiple labels for the same concept; terminology should be standardized.
Metaphors and conventions vary by culture; direct translation of English terminology often produces awkward or misleading text.

section.name: localization
section.p: Localization is deeper than word substitution:

list.item: Translate meaning, not just words
Adjust for cultural conventions
Date and number formats
Currency display
Reading direction (LTR/RTL)
Color meanings vary by culture

rule.statement: For multi-locale products, have a native speaker validate terminology in each locale independently — direct translation of English user-friendly terms can produce awkward or misleading text.
rule.rationale: What is plain language in one culture may be confusing or offended in another. Localization requires cultural expertise, not just language skill.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Claude subagents can audit a provided list of UI strings, error messages, and labels against a set of plain-language rules, generating a Language Audit Checklist with suggested replacements. Agents can also generate locale-appropriate copy variants when given a target locale and base English text. For systematic enforcement, agents can generate ESLint or custom linter rules that flag hard-coded technical strings.
section.note: Human review is required for domain-specific terminology where the "plain language" alternative changes meaning.

Recommended subagents: faion-usability-agent (language audit; generates plain-language replacements for technical UI strings); faion-sdd-executor-agent (implements terminology changes in code; updates i18n string files).

example.title: Language Audit Prompt
example.code:
Audit the following UI strings for violations of "Match Between System and Real World" (Nielsen #2). For each string: 1. Identify technical terms, jargon, or system-centric language 2. Suggest a plain-language alternative in the user's vocabulary 3. Flag if the meaning changes with the replacement (requires human review)
User type: {user_description} UI strings: {strings_list}

example.title: Error Message Rewrite Prompt

section.name: agent-gotchas
section.p: When working with agents on language matching, be aware of these failure modes:

antipattern.description: Agents rewriting technical terms often change meaning subtly
antipattern.reason: "Repository" replaced with "folder" loses the version-control concept. Require the agent to flag ambiguous replacements.

antipattern.description: Plain-language alternatives generated for generic instructions may not match the product's specific feature names
antipattern.reason: Provide a product glossary as context.

antipattern.description: Error message rewrites default to overly apologetic tone
antipattern.reason: Agents default to "We're so sorry something went wrong!" — specify the desired tone explicitly in the prompt.

antipattern.description: Agents do not know what terms your specific user segment actually uses
antipattern.reason: Without interview or support ticket data as input, they will use generic consumer-app vocabulary. Provide research data as context.

antipattern.description: Publishing agent-generated terminology without human review
antipattern.reason: Human checkpoint required before terminology changes are deployed — wrong plain-language replacements erode trust faster than technical jargon.

section.name: cli-tools-and-services
section.p: Tools for language auditing and style enforcement:

list.item: write-good: Lints prose for passive voice, weasel words, jargon (npm i -g write-good)
alex: Flags insensitive or unequal language in text (npm i -g alex; alexjs.com)
textlint: Extensible text linting; custom rules for domain jargon (npm i -g textlint; textlint.github.io)
vale: Prose style linter for technical writing; custom vocab lists (brew install vale; vale.sh)
i18next-parser: Extract i18n keys from code for audit (npm i -g i18next-parser)

section.p: SaaS and OSS services for language and localization:

list.item: Hemingway Editor (SaaS): Grade-level readability check; web-only
Readable.io (SaaS, Partial): Readability API; Flesch-Kincaid scoring
Vale + GitHub Actions (OSS, Yes): CI prose linting; enforce custom style guide in PRs
Lokalise (SaaS, Yes): i18n management with API; stores locale string variants
Phrase (Memsource) (SaaS, Yes): Translation management; API for string extraction
Crowdin (SaaS, Yes): OSS-friendly localization platform with API

section.name: inline-script
section.p: Scan i18n JSON for technical terms using a blocklist:

list.item: Source plain-language alternatives from support tickets and user interview transcripts, not from the team's internal vocabulary
Apply terminology decisions to a shared glossary first, then update the codebase — consistent terminology across the product is more important than any single perfect label
Error messages are the highest-ROI target: they occur at moments of user frustration and must be maximally clear
Test terminology with a comprehension task, not a preference question — ask "what would you do after reading this?" not "which version do you prefer?"
Maintain a two-column forbidden-terms list: left column system term, right column user term; feed this list to the linter and the agent's prompt

template.purpose: Language audit checklist for reviewing terminology, error messages, labels, and instructions
